Reference class: a major US equity index being above its current level after roughly 1 week, 1 month, and 3 months. Outside view, stock indices have a modest positive drift, so the chance of being higher than a starting point is usually a bit above 50%, unlike the generic FRED base rate. Starting from the 0.422 generic prior, I adjust upward because the DJIA is a risk asset with long-run upward drift. The latest known level before cutoff (47,632 on 2025-10-30) suggests the market was still elevated/strong into early November 2025, which supports slightly-better-than-even odds over short horizons. Offsetting that, the level is already high, and over these horizons volatility, valuation risk, Fed/macro surprises, and simple mean reversion can easily dominate drift. I therefore keep all forecasts close to 50%, with the farthest date closest to 0.5 to reflect greater uncertainty. Low confidence because I cannot use external search here and the reference date is a weekend, which creates some ambiguity about the exact comparison observation.

Reference class: short-horizon directional moves in a market-based short rate (the 1-year secondary-market T-bill yield) relative to a nearby anchor date. For this reference class, the outside-view base rate is closer to a coin flip than the generic 0.422 FRED prior, because daily/weekly yield direction is often near-random absent a strong regime signal. The question-specific signal we do have is that the latest known reading before the forecast date was 3.450 on 2025-10-30, which suggests short rates had already fallen substantially from earlier tightening-cycle highs and may still have had a mild easing/downward bias by early November 2025. That pushes me a bit below 50% for an increase versus 2025-11-09. Because forecast uncertainty should increase with horizon, I pull later probabilities back toward 0.5. So: slightly less than even odds of being higher one week later, still slightly less than even by early December, and close to a coin flip by early February.

I start from the generic FRED base rate of 0.422, but this question is much narrower: an administered ECB policy rate, over short horizons, relative to a reference date very near the forecast date. The latest known deposit facility rate as of 2025-10-30 was 2.000. ECB policy rates usually change only at Governing Council meetings, and on institutional grounds it is very unlikely there would be an intermeeting hike within a week or by early December. That makes the first two dates close to floor probabilities. By 2026-02-07, there is materially more time for at least one or two regular policy meetings and for inflation or financial conditions to shift, so the probability of being above the 2025-11-09 level rises, but a hike still seems distinctly less likely than hold/cut given the already-low 2.0 level and the apparent late-cycle/easing context implied by that level.

Reference class: short-horizon directional changes in an administered overnight policy rate (EFFR) relative to a nearby anchor date. I start from the generic FRED prior of 0.422, but this is a much more special case: EFFR usually moves mainly when the Fed changes its target range, and otherwise only by small technical fluctuations. The prompt’s last known value before the cutoff was 4.120 on 2025-10-30, implying policy was already around a lower range than 2024 highs, so the broad macro context looks more like hold-or-cut than renewed hiking. For 2025-11-16 and 2025-12-09, there is no scheduled FOMC meeting between 2025-11-09 and those dates, so an increase would most likely require minor market-technical drift or an unscheduled move; that keeps both below 50%, especially the first date. By 2026-02-07 there are additional scheduled meetings, so the chance of being above the 2025-11-09 reading rises, but I still keep it below 50% because the apparent rate regime already looked post-peak and a hike path was not the modal case. I also slightly discount the near dates because the comparison dates are Sundays, which likely maps to adjacent business-day observations and reduces room for change.

Starting from the stated empirical base rate of 0.422 for FRED directional questions, I shade downward for this specific series. NDR12MCD is a slow-moving, monthly, weighted-average administered deposit rate; over short horizons, 'no change' is common and counts as NO. So I put the Dec 9 probability below 40%, and the Feb 7 probability a bit closer to 50% but still below it.

Reference class: monthly interest-rate FRED series, especially deposit/savings-rate series that move slowly and are heavily influenced by the rate cycle.
